coredata.dc:title: New nano-composite based on carbon dots (CDots) decorated magnesium oxide (MgO) nano-particles (CDots@MgO) sensor for high H2S gas sensitivity performance
coredata.dc:creator: El-Shamy, Ahmed Gamal
coredata.dc:description:
Schottky device based on carbon dots (CDots) decorated magnesium oxide (MgO) nano-particles (CDots@MgO) has been engineered for H2S gas sensing applications. TEM microscope proves the decoration of CDots on the surface of MgO nano-particles. The sensor device has been tested for various reducing gases. However, CDots@MgO device displays high response against H2S gas, as a resultof reducing the barrier height between CDots@MgO crystals. I–V behavior of the engineered sensor device is examined under both open air and H2S gas environments. The parameters (series resistance Rs, effective barrier height
ϕ B
, and ideality factor n) of the schottky diode are determined for the CDots@MgO and MgO based sensors. Interestingly, under the exposure of 120 ppm of H2S, CDots@MgO sensor has shown response current value 11 times higher than MgO sensor at external biasing voltage = −0.7 V. The reduction of barrier height can be observed with increasing the (Tapplied ) up to 200 °C and then increases. The obtained results prove that both device response and sensitivity are strongly influenced by the change in the barrier height. Finally, this effort introduces an invaluable methodology aiming at sensing the harmful gases by engineering a new gas sensor based on Schottky device of metal oxides decorated with CDots for high response, low power-consumption, high repeatability and high stability.

head.ce:title: New nano-composite based on carbon dots (CDots) decorated magnesium oxide (MgO) nano-particles (CDots@MgO) sensor for high H2S gas sensitivity performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physics Department, Faculty of Science, Suez Canal University, Ismailia, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Physics Department, Faculty of Science, Suez Canal University, Ismailia, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Schottky device based on carbon dots (CDots) decorated magnesium oxide (MgO) nano-particles (CDots@MgO) has been engineered for H2S gas sensing applications. TEM microscope proves the decoration of CDots on the surface of MgO nano-particles. The sensor device has been tested for various reducing gases. However, CDots@MgO device displays high response against H2S gas, as a resultof reducing the barrier height between CDots@MgO crystals. I–V behavior of the engineered sensor device is examined under both open air and H2S gas environments. The parameters (series resistance Rs, effective barrier height
ϕ B
, and ideality factor n) of the schottky diode are determined for the CDots@MgO and MgO based sensors. Interestingly, under the exposure of 120 ppm of H2S, CDots@MgO sensor has shown response current value 11 times higher than MgO sensor at external biasing voltage = −0.7 V. The reduction of barrier height can be observed with increasing the (Tapplied ) up to 200 °C and then increases. The obtained results prove that both device response and sensitivity are strongly influenced by the change in the barrier height. Finally, this effort introduces an invaluable methodology aiming at sensing the harmful gases by engineering a new gas sensor based on Schottky device of metal oxides decorated with CDots for high response, low power-consumption, high repeatability and high stability.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, gas sensor devices have been capturing the attention of the scientific community due to their wide range of applications [1]. Indeed, hydrogen sulfide H2S gas is irritant, flammable, corrosive, highly toxic even at parts per million (ppm) concentrations and it is produced from the decomposition of organic materials [2]. There are many sources for producing H2S gas like paper milling, industrial oils and/or gases, and sewage treatment [3]. The exposure to H2S gas is often considered a high risk threatening the health of employees in the industrial sector. Also, H2S is extensively used in chemical laboratiers, being is colorless, tasteless, and odorless, as well as it is very difficult to be detected [4]. Exposure to high H2S concentrations (100 ppm) leads to an immediate collapse with loss of breathing and high probability of death [5]. Therefore, it is vital to detect H2S gas at low concentrations. So, the predictable risks of H2S gas and the requirements to sensors with higher H2S sensitivity and lower power-consumption drive the researchers and academics to innovate and develop new materials with the ability to satisfy these desires. The high selectivity, rapid recovery time, long term stability, high response, and low working temperature are the essential requirements for a good H2S gas sensor [6]. Recently, the development of H2S gas sensors has increased to fulfill the desired requirements to high performance sensors. Actually, to achieve these goals, a lot of materials have been introduced. Also many approaches and techniques have been presented to detect H2S gas, for instance, optical mass spectrometry, gas chromatography, electrochemical, piezoelectric sensors, and other instruments [7]. But these instruments/devices have some disadvantages being expensive, malfunctioning in harsh environment, less stable, high power consuming, inflexible, bulky structure, limited usage in the in-situ and continuous measurements, no real-time detection and also their difficult operability [2,8,9]. So, the development of gas sensors has been extensively progressing in the scientific community. Therefore, semiconducting metal oxides nanostructures (such as TiO2, WO3, SnO2, and others) have been extensively applied in the gas sensing applications [10] due to their unique properties like large surface area- to- volume ratios, high thermal and chemical stabilities, ease preparation, low cost, and excellent sensing properties [11]. Numbers of experiments have been developed for increasing the sensing features of metal oxides by engineering different structures such as nano-particles, nano-sheets, nano-rods, thin films and others [12–14]. In spite of the tuning and modulation of the metal oxide structure, weak selectivity, small surface reactions, high activation energy, large depletion layer, and weak charge carrier separation, are still the great limitations for these metal oxides [9,11]. To overcome these limitations, various materials (transition and noble metals [15–19]) were used to increase the sensing features by doping the metal oxide particles. Indeed, once the noble metals are doped with the metal oxide particles, the sensing properties were improved by using the effect of catalytic behavior of noble metals to reduce the activation energy of the metal oxide particles [20]. However, some shortcomings such as the catalytic poisoning (reduction in activity) from definite gases and the high preparation cost have been appeared by the use of noble metals, besides, at higher operating temperatures the metals tend to form the coarsen and clusters [21]. Additionally, gas sensing features can be enhanced by doping the hybrid metal oxide material with third materials. This method is considered another effective method by enhancing the number of defects in the structure of the crystal or altering the electrical behavior of the hybrid metal oxide semiconductor structures [22]. However, the device doesn't work with its maximum efficiency in case of using the deposition approach to synthesize; this is attributed to the deposition of noble metal on only one side of the sensing material [23]. In fact, many literatures have been introduced on metal oxides alone or metal oxides doped with other materials for sensing H2S gas. In addition to all the above limitations, the higher operating temperature is another great issue for the metal oxide gas sensors, which means the high power-consumption of the sensor [24]. For example, Kim et al. [25] reported on the CuO nanorods for sensing 100 ppm of H2S gas with a respose 4 at 300 °C. Mortezaali et al. [26] reported on the ZnO thin film for sensing 20 ppm of H2S gas with a respose 2 at 500 °C. Shi et al. [27] reported on the 3D rGO/h-WO3 nanocomposite for sensing 1 ppm of H2S gas with a respose 10.80 at 330 °C. Also, Sun et al. [4] reported on the decoration of NiO with Fe2O3 nano-particles for sensing 200 ppm of H2S gas with a response 26.55 at 300 °C.

Generally, there are many structures reported on the H2S gas sensing based on the interface. In fact, n-type and p-type semiconductor metal oxides have been applied for H2S gas sensing. Metal oxides with n-type semiconductor structure are extensively applied in the gas sensing applications than p-type metal oxides thanks to many advantages like their higher stability in low oxygen atmospheres and response compatibility to measuring devices [28]. However, recently, many advantages were reported for p-type metal oxides like (i) they are good catalysts which help in the selective oxidation of H2S gas (ii) high solubility of oxygen (high absorption of oxygen) in low humid conditions and hence rapid recovery [29]. Based on the benefits of these semiconducting types of metal oxides, the combination between n-n/p-p and p–n nano-junctions have appeared to enhance the sensing performance [30]. While the electrons can be simply transferred to the conduction bands (lower-energy orbitals) at the n-n/p-p junction interface, creating “accumulation layers” rather than a depletion layers [11]. On the metal oxides surface, Oxygen is adsorbed and leads to depleting the accumulation layers, and at the interface, the potential energy barrier increases, thus increasing the response. Alternatively, in the p-n nano-junctions, the n-type is decorated with p-type metal oxides and vice-vera, producing a depletion layers at the interfaces. Electrons transfer from n-type though the interface to the available holes. Depletion layers are produced thanks to the e−/h+ recombination on both p–n junction sides, producing a potential barrier for electron flow. When applying H2S, a large decrease in the depletion layers and potential barrier occurred resulting in declining resistance and improving the response [11].

Inspired by these structures, we present a new structure based on (metal oxide/Si) Schottky diode. In this structure, the metal oxides either n-type or p-type are deposited on the Si wafer substrate (p- type or n-type) to produce a familiar Schottky diode.

In fact, the author expects great advantages for this structure as an H2S gas sensor such as (i) every atom of the metal oxides can form a p-n junction with a Si substrate. (ii) Every atom of the metal oxides can form n-n and/or p-p junctions with the adjacent metal oxides atom. (iii) Increasing the adsorption surface area, increasing the area of reaction, and also increasing the exposure sensing area by depositing the metal oxide uniformly on the Si substrate. (iv) Using the traditional metal oxides and avoiding the difficulty in selecting the appropriate decorating metal oxide to p-n or n-n/p-p junctions. (v) Avoiding the creation of undesired structures (single phase and/or binary phase structure at higher temperature) which can produce a conflict in the response. (vi) Large (e−/h+) pairs separation by blocking the (e−/h+) pairs recombination. (vii) Use the benfits of the Schottky diode such as the operating on forward and reverse bias. All these expected advantages give this structure the opportunity to be a good H2S gas sensor.

Thus, keeping in minds the benfits and shortcomings, we introduce high sensitive, high response, low operating temperature sensors based on carbon dots (CDots) decorating magnesium oxide (MgO) nanoparticles, where MgO is a base material in the engineered sensors. In fact, MgO nanostructure as a semiconctor with a wide band gap can be efficiently used in many applications such as adsorption, photocatalysts, refractory, paint, waste remediation, pharmaceutical industry, and removal of toxic gases [31] due to its high surface area, its defect structure, high thermal and chemical stabilities, easy preparation, low cost, nontoxicity, high mechanical strength, and the facile performance of the complex reactions in the environmental conditions [32]. These preoperties make MgO a promising candidate for gas sensing. Also, CDots nanoparticles have been appeared as a unique materials which insert in many applications such as catalysys, adsorptions, photocatalysts, thermoelectrics, shielding materials [33–35] due to their excellent optical, electrical and electronic properties [36–38].

On the other hand, we claimed that there are no literatures reported on using MgO only for H2S sensing, based on the best of our survey. However, Hashishin et al. [39] used Magnesium ferrite to detect H2S gas. They found that the sensor respositivity didn't improve when applying the excess of MgO in MgFe2O4 matrix, due to increasing the insulating behavior of the sensor with MgO. Although the increasing number of reports on enhancing the gas sensing features of the materials, achieving high gas sensitivity, short time response, low operating temperature and high selectivity still remains essential requirements.

However, we claim that no literatures reported on CDots@MgO schottky sensor device for H2S gas sensing until now based on the best of our survey. MgO nano-structure was selected here because of its high catalytic performance, converting energy to the chemical one, easy preparation, low price, availability, and its high resistance to fire and corrosion. In addition, the decoration of MgO with CDots nano-particles was done to increase the conductivity of MgO structure, increase the stability, and enhance the device response.

In this paper, we introduce a new Schottky device based on carbon dots decorated magnesium oxide nano-particles (CDots@MgO) for H2S gas sensing applications. The Schottky device was built up by coating (CDots@MgO) on a p-Si wafer by using a spin coater. CDots@MgO device has higher sensitivity and response to H2S gas than (MgO) device. Excitingly, the decoration of CDots nano-particles on MgO surface makes the device work on the reverse bias connection. Under the exposure of 120 ppm of H2S gas, CDots@MgO sensor displayed response current value 11 times higher than MgO sensor at external biasing voltage = −0.7 V. Moreover, the Schottky barrier height of the sensor device was extensively studied together with its relation with the sensor response and sensitivity of H2S. The mechanism and the gas selectivity were also reported. In addition, the impact of the relative humidity and applied temperature (Tapplied ) on the sensor was performed. It was found that the H2S response (99.6 %) was detected at 200 °C and the response (92.2 %) was detected at 30 °C. However, this study was performed 30 °C and the reason behind that is the introducing of a new H2S gas sensor with avoiding the high operating temperature, especially the sensor possesses the large response at 30 °C. Finally, this (CDots@MgO) sensor shows a high sensitivity against the harmful gas (H2S) by engineering a new gas sensor based on Schottky device of metal oxides decorated with CDots for high response, sensitivity, low power-consumption and high stability.
Magnesium nitrate dehydrate (99.9 %), Potassium hydroxide (99.8 %), Hydrogen Fluoride (HF), Ethanol (CH3CH2OH, 99.9 %) and d-glucose (99.9 %), were achieved from (El-Nasr Chemical Co., Egypt).

The hydrothermal approach was used to synthesize MgO nano-particles [40] as follow (Scheme 1): Solution (A) prepared by dissolving 1.25 mmol of Mg(NO3)2·6H2O in 25 mL of de-ionized (DI) water at ambient temperature. Solution (B) was prepared by dissolving 1.0 g of KOH in 100 mL of (DI) water. Then, 25 mL of solution B which was injected into the above solution (A) with a rate (1 drop/sec) under mechanical stirring. Then, the obtained solution was moved to a 100 mL autoclave and heated for 3 h at 180 °C. The precipitates were collected after cooling it to room temperature. Then, the obtained powder rinsed many times with DI water and ethanol before drying at 60 °C for 24 h.
The microwave heating method was applied to create carbon dot nano-particles (CDots) as reported in our previous literatures [41–46].

CDots@MgO nano-particles were created as a follow (Scheme 1): MgO was suspended in ethanolic solution, which was prepared by dissolving 50 mg of MgO in 25 mL of ethanol, and mixed at ambient temperature with 15 μL of solution containing (CDots) nano-particles by using a mechanical stirrer for 60 min. The final product was centrifuged, filtered and washed many times with ethanol and then air-dried.

To engineer the schottky device sensor, CDots@MgO and/or MgO nano-particles were mixed with a small amount of de-ionized water to get a paste and then coated on a p-Si wafer as follow (Scheme 1): 20 mg of CDots@MgO and/or MgO nano-particles were mixed with 5 mL of de-ionized water. The obtained solution was spin coated onto a p-Si wafer by using a spin coater with rotation rate (2000 rpm) for 5 min. Then, the device was dried at 80 °C for 30 min in furnace to evaporate and remove the residual organic compounds and solvent. To obtain the targeted thickness (100 ± 10 nm), the above procedures were repeated many times (Scheme 1). After that, the two electrodes of the device were painted with Ag paste with a contact area 3.14 × 10−2 cm2 to make a schottky sensor device. A p-Si (100) wafer was washed with a mixture solution prepared from acetone, ethanol and hydrogen fluoride (HF) for about 15 min in ultrasonic bath container to clean it. Then, these p-Si wafers were washed, cleaned with DI water and dried in the existence of N2.

All of the prepared samples were characterized by XRD diffractometer (Brucker Axs-D8) with a monochromatic beam of a wavelength 0.154 nm Cu Kα radiation. The samples were measured at the 2θ range between 10° and 90° at the room temperature. Also, the particle size and the location of CDots on MgO surface were explored by using TEM microscope, Model JEOL-JEM-1230 at 120 kV, by dropping the prepared solution into a carbon grid. The morphology of the prepared film surface was analyzed by using A (JEOL JSM- 6500 F) SEM microscope. A BET instrument was applied to explore the absorption-adsorption behavior and porosity of the nano-particles under a steam of liquid Nitrogen. The BET Model (Micromeritics ASAP 2020, USA) was conducted in this experiment.

For the measurements of schottky device parameters (ideality factor n, effective barrier height
ϕ B
, and series resistance Rs), Keithley 6514 Electrometer was used to measure the I–V behavior. The I–V measurement of the schottky device sensor was measured in the open air environment and the H2S environment. The device was placed into airtight glass chamber with capacity 3 × 103 cm3. The desired concentrations of gases (10 ppm – 120 ppm) were injected through this chamber by using a mass flow rate which the total flow rate is kept at 500 mL min−1. The measurements were done at the temperature =30 °C. All gas sensing experiments were done under a relative humidity of (50 ± 5%) and atmospheric pressure. Also, the device was tested for different operating voltages V= - 0.5 V, - 0.7 V and - 1 V. The response of the fabricated sensor device through gas detection was evaluated from the relation;
(1)
S sensitivity
=
(
G gas
-
G air
)
G air
Where the conductance of the device in the open air is denoted by Gair at 30 °C and the conductance of the device in gas environment is denoted by Ggas at 30 °C. Also, the "sensitivity" of the device was evaluated by the slope of the calibration curve, that is, sensor response versus target gas concentration (10 ppm – 120 ppm). The impact of the relative humidity (R.H% = 10, 25, 45, 55, 70 and 90 %) on the response of the sensor was performed. Under a heating rate 10 °C/min, the deposited film has been heated at various applied temperatures Tapplied = 100, 200, 400 and 800 °C for 1 h to measure the effect of temperature on the performance of the sensor device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization (XRD, TEM and SEM) of the samples
ce:section.ce:para: Fig S1 shows the XRD pattern of CDots nanoparticles. As observed, a broad hump at 2θ = 23° was detected for C(002) plane of CDots [33–38]. (Fig. 1) presents XRD analysis of MgO and CDots@MgO nano-particles. As seen, XRD pattern of MgO nano-particles (Fig. 1a) was detected and it shows that there are five XRD patterns with (hkl) planes (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) for 2θ = 30.2°, 37.6°, 60.6°, 75.9° and 80.4° respectively, which describe the crystalline phase of a cubic system of MgO, according to (JCPDS Card No. 87-0653, MgO) [47].

Fig. 1b shows XRD patterns of CDots@MgO nano-particles. It was observed that the crystalline phase of MgO was not affected by coating the surface of MgO with CDots. Moreover, CDots pattern was detected at 2θ = 23° in the CDots@MgO nano-composite that proves the decoration of the MgO surface with CDots. A weak pattern detected for CDots is attributed to the small amount of CDots [33–38]. Morever, the peak positions were shifted to higher angles 2θ = 33.2°, 40.5°, 62.6°, 77.5° and 82.3° for the CDots@MgO sample. This is attributed to the lattice strain due to the decoration of CDots on the surface of MgO [48].

TEM micrographs were studied to explore the size and shape of the nanoparticles. Fig S2 shows the TEM micrograph of the CDots nanoparticles. From the first sight, it is of great importance to mention that CDots nano-particles have a spherical and regular shape with a homogenous structure (Fig S2). The particle size of these CDots nano-particles was statistically estimated and it ranged from 2 to 10 nm with a mean size about 5.4 ± 1 nm. This value matches with the particle size value (3.42 nm) in Refs. [33–38]. On the other hand, TEM micrographs for MgO and CDots@MgO nano-particles were shown in (Fig. 1c,d) Fig. 1c shows TEM micrograph of MgO particles and it was found that the particle size of MgO lies in the nano-scale range and they also have a spherical shape with a uniform distribution and arrangement (like isolated islands). Also, the average particle size is statistically estimated and it was found around ∼ 24 ± 1 nm. This proves that there is no agglomeration, which means the success of hydrothermal method in preparation of MgO nano-particles without any agglomeration. But it is clear that there was a slight change in the particle size (∼ 28 ± 2 nm) detected when CDots decorated the surface of MgO (Fig. 1d). Also, the micrograph had clearly shown the decoration of CDots on the surface of MgO. It can be said that the change in the particle size (∼ 5 nm) within the CDots size. This behavior was observed in many literatures [43,35]. These observations were confirmed by FE-SEM measurements.

FE-SEM micrographs were performed to examine the surface morphology of MgO and CDots@MgO (Fig. 1e,f). The spherical structure of the as prepared MgO nano-particles was observed with a uniform distribution. Also, the particle size of the as prepared MgO nano-particles is smaller than 50 nm (Fig. 1e). On the other hand, when CDots decorated the surface of MgO, it was found that the morphology remained without any change (shape and size). However, some dense structures were observed because of the decoration process (Fig. 1f) which help in increasing the adsorption of gas. In addition to, the spherical shape of CDots@MgO nano-particles, they also have a regular shape. As shown from the micrographs, MgO and CDots@MgO nano-particles are well distributed and their particle sizes lie in a nano-scale with an average size smaller than 50 nm.

In this work, two sensors based on (MgO) and (CDots@MgO) have been engineered, to compare between their performances and investigate the role of CDots in the performance of the gas sensor. The sensing performance for both sensors was done in the open air and H2S gas environments at temperature 30 °C.

(Fig. 3a,b) shows the J–V behavior of MgO device sensor in the open air conditions. The rectifying behavior was clearly observed with a rectification ratio 40 at applied voltage Vbiasing = ±1 V. This rectification behavior was observed due to the schottky junction formed at the interfaces between MgO crystals and Si substrate [49].

From the forward bias of I–V behavior, it is easy to determine the parameters of the Schottky device. These parameters are ideality factor, series resistance and effective barrier height and can be represented as n, Rs and
ϕ B
, respectively. In fact, the Schottky diode can be submitted in the explanation to the model of thermo-ionic emission and can be stated by (Eq. 2) [49].

Where the reverse saturation current is denoted by (Is), Boltzmann’s constant is denoted by (k), the temperature is denoted by (T in Kelvin), Richardson’s constant is denoted by (A*), area of the device is denoted by (A) and the bias voltage applied on the diode is denoted by (V), respectively. Where the value of Is is calculated at V equals zero, from the extrapolation of the relation between log I and V. From the above relations, the barrier height and ideality factor of the sensor (MgO) were calculated based on the thermo-ionic approach and they were found to be 0.55 eV and 2.7, respectively. Noticeably, the measurements of the I–V behavior for the sensors were performed at 30 °C.

Also, Norde proposed the first method for calculating the value of Rs for diode with (n) equals one (ideal Schottky diode). Then Norde’s approach was modified by Sato and Yasumura to calculate the parameters of diode and Rs for diode with (n) larger than one (the non-ideal Schottky diode) from the forward bias of the (I–V) plot. However, both approaches need to measure the (I–V) behavior at two different temperatures. So, in this work, the diode parameters and Rs were calculated by using Cheung and Cheung method [51].

The diode parameters (Rs and n) were obtained from the slope and the y-axis intercept of the relation between dV/d(ln I) and I, which is a linear relation (Eq. 6). Then, by using the calculated value of ideality factor (n) in Eq. 8, also the relation between H(I) and current (I) produces a linear relation with intercept of y-axis. Both the slope and intercept with y-axis refer to (n
ϕ B
) and Rs values, respectively. The term dV/d(lnI) versus current (I) for the (MgO) sensor was plotted and depicted in (Fig. 3c). From the Eq. (6), the n and Rs values were obtained from the Cheung approach and were found to be 2.9 and 15.1 Ω. Also, the term H(I) versus current (I) was plotted and depicted in (Fig. 3d). From the Eq. (8), the
ϕ B
and Rs values for the same sensor were obtained from the Cheung approach and were found to be 0.56 eV and 15.3 Ω. It is clear that the n and
ϕ B
values that obtained by the thermo-ionic and Cheung approach are approximately the same. Also the Rs values that obtained from the two Eqs. (6 and 8) are approximately the same, which confirm the reliability of Cheung’s approach.

On the other hand, the J–V behavior of (CDots@MgO) sensor in open air conditions at 30 °C is depicted in (Fig. 4a,b). Noteworthy, the sensor exhibited an excellent schottky behavior and the rectification ratio was improved by 1.3 times higher than the sensor without CDots decoration, where the rectification ratio of (CDots@MgO) sensor was about 55 at the applied voltage Vbiasing = ±1 V. Also, the parameters (n,
ϕ B
, Rs) of the schottky diode were obtained from the Cheung approach and were found to be n = 2.2,
ϕ B
=0.69 eV and Rs = 163.5 Ω. It is clear that the ideality factors for both sensors deviate from the value (n = 1), due to the existence of the trap levels at the surface of MgO nano-particles. Also, the existence of CDots on the surface of MgO leads to MgO surface modification and hence enhancement in the barrier height
ϕ B
for (CDots@MgO) sensor. Moreover, the Rs value of the same sensor is found to be higher than (MgO) sensor, due to decoration of CDots nano-particles.

The J–V behavior of (CDots@MgO) sensor under the open air and H2S gas environments at temperature 30 °C is depicted in (Fig. 5a). Also, the J–V behavior of (MgO) sensor in the open air and H2S gas environments at a temperature 30 °C is depicted in (Fig. 5b). Excitingly, under H2S gas environments the sensor (CDots@MgO) shows a change in reverse saturation current higher than (MgO) sensor. For instance, at the applied voltage Vbiasing = −1 V the current change is 13.5 mA/cm2 for the CDots decorated sensor and 1.1 mA/cm2 for the sensor without CDots decoration. Discussion of this behavior is attributed to the dissociation of oxygen molecules in air environment under the experimental (ambient) conditions, and each atom of oxygen to complete its bond, it has to receive an electron from MgO [11]. Only, oxygen is adsorbed at MgO surface, as a result, the electrons which are only removed to a certain depth (Debye length, typically having the oeder λ ∼ 2–100 nm) from the surface, producing a depletion region [52]. This leads to reducing the electron density in MgO, producing a high barrier height and the high resistance of MgO and hence decreasing the current in air environment (Fig. 5c). This process is submitted to the following relation [11],
R =
R o
exp
q
V b
kT
where R and R0 are the resistance and initial resistance of the sensing material, q is the electron charge, k is Boltzmann’s constant, Vb is the potential energy barrier height, and T is the temperature in kelvin. In fact, the Debye length is responsible for the alteration in the resistance/conduction by the adsorption of oxygen (more or less amount) on MgO surface. However, when (H2S) gas is applied as a reducing gas, it covers all MgO nano-strustures having size within the Debye length [11]. This facilitates the strong interaction between the applied H2S gas and the surface adsorbed oxygen; as a result H2s gas pulls oxygen from MgO surface and concurrently donates an electron back into MgO which makes/opens conduction path channels of electrons that cause a decrease in the barrier height, resistance and hence increasing the current in H2S gas environment [4]. Moreover, decorating the surface of MgO with CDots enhances the absorption-adsorption of H2S molecules, more reaction with the surface adsorbed oxygen and more donating of electrons back into MgO, producing a huge decrease in the barrier height, resistance and hence a high increase in the current in H2S gas environment (Fig. 5d).

The current response (JH2S − Jair) for the two sensors under the open air conditions and (120 ppm) H2S gas environments at the applied voltage Vbiasing = −0.7 V is depicted in Fig. 6 . It is obvious that the (JH2S − Jair) values of the sensor (CDots@MgO) is 20.1 mA/cm2 and for the sensor (MgO) is 2.1 mA/cm2 at the same condition. These values demonstrate the significant role that CDots nano-particles play in achieving higher conductive behaviors of MgO based Schottky junction. This behavior is ascribed to the trap levels on MgO surface that induce the carriers injected into MgO nano-particles. In open air conditions, at the MgO surface, the adsorbed O2 molecules arrest the free electrons found in MgO creating a low-conductivity depletion layer close to the surfaces. However, In the H2S gas environments, unpaired electrons are generated in the MgO just when the generated holes move to the surface and are trapped, these unpaired electrons share in maximize the current.

(Scheme 2) shows the mechanism of the conduction thanks to the trapping of holes at the surface of MgO. As seen, the ambient molecules of (O2) were adsorbed at the surface of the metal oxide semiconductor MgO owing to the arrest of free electrons,
(9)
O
2
gas
↔
O
2
adsorbed
(10)
O
2
adsorbed
+
e −
↔
O
2
adsorbed
−

Consequently, at the surface of MgO, the adsorbed O2 molecules create a depletion layer (Scheme 2). So, the pairs of (e−/h+) are created under H2S injection, and also due to the band bending, the holes (h+) move to the surface of MgO and as a result, oxygen ions (with -ve charge) will release [O2 − (adsorption) + h+→ O2(gas)] (Scheme 2). As a result, the current increases, due to the collection of unpaired electrons at the electrodes. Also, the response current improves according to the increase in the high density of trap states due to the hole-trapping produced by adsorption-desorption of oxygen at the surface of MgO. In fact, hole-trapping takes place at the interface of metal − semiconductor of the Schottky junction under the reverse bias connection which makes the depletion region shrink. Owing to the thinning/thin thickness of the of depletion layer, the electron tunneling behavior takes place after specific reverse bias voltage. Thus when the tunneling electron passes more than one time, this way leads to increasing the current. In fact, MgO has a high sensitivity to absorb and adsorb H2S molecules and also CDots decorating the surface of MgO boost the absorption-adsorption of H2S molecules, this produces an efficient hole trapping and decreases the recombination of electron − holes. Hence, the current increases. In fact, the CDots serve as the electrons donor/receptor and this leads to pulling the electrons from MgO. The main explanation is; (i) both MgO and CDots lead to separating the generated (e–/h+) pairs. The (e–/h+) separation takes place by hindering the charge recombination to create reactive sites. The generated reactive sites act as an electron reservoir [34]. When the (e–/h+) pairs are created, the energy levels ease the electrons transfer from the MgO conduction bands to the adjacent CDots levels, and this helps in separating the (e–/h+) pairs that were created by applying H2S gas through hindering the charge recombination and creating the reactive sites [34]. (ii) Indeed, there are great energy differences between the MgO conduction bands and the CDots work functions which lead to increasing the capability of the strong electronic coupling between MgO conduction bands and π electrons in CDots, creating the up-conversion and extending the ability of absorbing H2S gas and hence inceasing the current [34,53].

Also, another and an important parameter required for the high quality and energy efficient gas sensor is the operating bias voltage. The greater operating bias voltage (Vbiasing) needs more and more power for gas sensor operating. In our present work, the higher sensitivity of H2S gas behavior and the higher response current were obtained at the applied voltage = −1 V for the (CDots@MgO) Schottky junction sensor. This means that the less operating bias voltage (Vbiasing) is one of the most important features of this sensor device [49].

The response current (JH2S-Jair) of the sensors was studied at different concentrations of H2S gas at applied voltage Vbiasing = -1 V and depicted in (Fig. 7a). As seen, the response current increases with increasing the amount/concentration of H2S gas at the constant biasing voltage. More interestingly, the current shows stability under the same concentration of H2S during the same run. Moreover, the response current shows no change at forward bias under the same condition with varying H2S concentration (Fig. 7b). More noteworthy, the value of response current (JH2S − Jair) at 80 ppm for the (CDots@MgO) sensor improves by 8.4 times higher than the (MgO) sensor, which may be due to the strong adsorption property of CDots with H2S gas. Also, (Fig. 7c) shows the response current (change in the current density, JH2S − Jair) for both CDots@MgO and MgO sensors for different H2S concentrations at the applied voltage -5 V.

The sensitivity of the device sensor was studied by measuring the response of the sensor (Ggas-Gair/Gair) against different concentration of H2S (10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100 and 120 ppm), respectively (Fig. 8 a). The diode works at Vbiasing= - 1.0 V and temperature =30 °C. It was found that, at the whole sensing concentration range (10−120 ppm), the sensor displays a high respone to the different concentration of H2S gas. It was observed that the sensing response increases with increasing H2S concentration. The relation between the response and the concentration has a good linear fitting with the correlation coefficient R2 = 0.99754.

On the other hand, the impact of relative humidity (RH%) on the device sensor response was studied and depicted in (Fig. 8b). The sensor works at Vbiasing = - 1.0 V, temperature =30 °C and 120 ppm H2S gas. As seen, the obtained data show some stability in the sensor response when the RH = 15 %, and the device still display a higher respose (> 130 %) at RH = 25 %. It was observed that the device displayed a high response (> 100 %) with increasing the RH% to 45 %. For the RH% value above 45 %, a melodramatic decrease in the sensor response is observed. The decrease in the sensor response is predicted because of the water molecules are competed with oxygen for occupying the surface sites. With the growth of the RH% value, more water molecules are adsorbed so that density of the adsorbed oxygen is decreased producing a reduction in the sensor response. On the other hand, it was observed that at RH% value = 70 %, the sensing response of the sensor CDots@MgO to 120 ppm H2S is ∼74 ± 3%, which is still some higher value of response. This indicates that the sensor CDots@MgO is highly stable and working well in the humidity conditions. Also, the stability of CDots@MgO sensor with time under exposure to 120 ppm H2S gas was studied (Fig. 8c), The sensor works at Vbiasing = - 1.0 V, temperature =30 °C, R.H% = 50 ± 5% and interval time 5 days for 75 days. From the (Fig. 8c), the sensor shows stability for long-time continuous measurements, indicating that the CDots@MgO sensor has a good stability.

The repeatability of the CDots@MgO sensor was explored under the same environments (Fig. 8d). The tests were carried out for ten cycles with each cycle lasting for 30 min. the sensor displayed a high stability in its response for along eight cycles with a decrease by 5 % in its response after the tenth cycle. In fact, the reason behind the high stability is attributed to the coating of CDots on the MgO surfaces, where the CDots serve as the donor/receptor of electrons which can pull the electrons from MgO, producing a delay in the corrosion reactions and hence increasing the CDots@MgO sensor stabilities [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature on CDots@MgO sensor for H2S detection
ce:section.ce:para:
As seen, the lowering in barrier height is limited to 200 °C and 400 °C for the corresponding sensors and this can be explained by the effect of temperature on the grain/particle size and its relation with the adsorption mechanism of H2S gas. In fact at high temperature (800 °C), the grain size increases to be 135 ± 7 nm. The large grain size means that a small surface area is exposed to react with H2S gas. This leads to an increase in the depletion layer which is formed by the adsorption of ionic oxygen (
O
2 −
, O– and
O 2 −
). The increase in the depletion layer leads to increasing the barrier height at high temperature, which is reflected in reducing the sensor response [4].

This behavior is supported by SEM images (Fig. 9c-f). It was found that with increasing the applied temperature, the particle/grain size of the CDots@MgO increases. The average grain sizes were estimated and it was found to be 30.8 ± 2, 52.5 ± 4, 84.5 ± 5 and 135 ± 7 nm for Tapplied = 100, 200, 400 and 800 °C, respectively. In fact, the grain size of CDots@MgO particles increases with increasing temperature and the large grains formed at the higher temperature. As a result, H2S adsorption rate will be reduced and these grains will not be completely exposed to gas.

To prove the above discussion, the performance of the annealed devices to response H2S gas was studied (Fig. 9b). It is shown that the device posses the highest response at temperature 200 °C for CDots@MgO. It can be concluded that, when the grain size increases and becomes large, the states at surface will trap the free electrons, thus the free carriers will not be able to reach the grains and the grains are depleted from it (Fig. 9g). So, the electron transportation will be inhibited by this depletion layer through the structure of the large grain size. Thus the response of device will be smaller. But the gas ions will fully cover the whole nano-crystallites in the small grain diameter case, which produces a melodramatic change in the device conduction. Thus the response of device will be larger. In fact, the small grain size characteristic creates CDots@MgO increasing the sensing response to H2S gas by the high surface-to-volume ratios. As a result, large active adsorption sites are available on the CDots@MgO surface for H2S gas modules. In fact, the strong competition between H2S and O2 was produced for adsorption on the CDots@MgO surfaces. Therefore when exposed to H2S, H2S can replace the heavily oxygen adsorbate due to the large differences in the adsorption energies between O2 and H2S to CDots@MgO surfaces, therefore the resistance of the sensor decreased and hence the sensor response increased [11].

From the study, it can be seen, the effect of CDots on enhancing the response of the sensor. Where decorating the surface of MgO with CDots enhances the absorption-adsorption of H2S molecules, more reaction with the surface adsorbed oxygen and more donating of electrons back into MgO, a huge decrease in the barrier height andresistance and leading to an increase in the current in H2S gas environment. The decoration of CDots on the MgO surface provides a high surface area, more and more active sites of adsorption and pore volumes which adsorb H2S gas on their surfaces. Also, CDots are highly sensitive to heat, thus facilitating the sensor response at low temperature [34].

Table 2 presents the comparison between the performance of our sensors and the previously reported H2S gas sensors. As seen, our sensors posses a high response at a low operating temperatures, low power-consumption, high stability for a long time and high repeatability as compared to the other sensors. The advantage of our sensor is attributed to (i) the engineering of the Schottky structure which enables the creation of p-n interface and p-p and/or n-n interfaces at the same structure. (ii) Enhancing the e−/h+ pairs separation and/or blocking of the e−/h+ pair recombination. (iii) The use of the Schottky diode benfits where the Schottky device can be operate in the forward and reverse bias and saving the operating energy. Moreover, in order to increase the sensing performance of our sensors, it is recommended that replacing the metal oxides with metal peroxides (MgO2 and/or ZnO2) due to its higher negative surface charge (electronegative behavior) [48] or replacing the CDots with graphene due to its layer structure and high conductivity [57,58].

In fact, one of the most important questions in this study is why H2S gas?. To answer this important question, the J–V behavior of MgO and CDots@MgO sensor devices was measured under the impact of 120 ppm of different gases at temperature 30 °C (Fig. 10 a,b). It was observed that the type of gas has a strong impact/effect on the value of current density. The Schottky barrier height was calculated and the values are depicted in Fig. 10c,d. The response of device for 120 ppm of the tested gases was evaluated and depicted in Fig. 10c,d. Concerning Fig. 10c,d, when the device is exposed to H2S gas, the device sensor recorded the lowest Schottky barrier height and the higher response value. This decline in the barrier height can be explained as follow: when the device is exposed to H2S gas, (
O 2 −
) ions will oxidize H2S gas and the electrons will release back to the conduction band of MgO. As well as the number of (
O 2 −
) ions on the surface of MgO decreases, this leads to a decrease in the thickness of depletion layer. Hence, the reduction of Schottky barrier is produced and it facilitates the traveling of electrons through MgO grains. It is clear that the device has a higher response to H2S gas as compared to other gases in this study. The response of MgO sensor was determined and it was found 51 %, 22 %, 11 % and 29 % for H2S, NH3, H2 and CH3OH, respectively. Also, the response of CDots@MgO sensor was determined and it was found 92 %, 52 %, 42 % and 71 % for H2S, NH3, H2 and CH3OH, respectively. This discrepancy in the response is attributed to the following chemical reactions;

α is 1 or 2 for description of singly or doubly ionized oxygen. Dominant oxygen species are molecular ions (
O 2 −
at T < 100 °C and
O 2
2 −
at 100 < T < 175 °C) at low temperatures and atomic ions (O− at 175 < T < 300 °C and
O
2 −
at T > 300 °C) at higher temperatures [59,60]. In our case, the sensor works at temperature 30 °C. In fact, gases easily react with the adsorbed oxygen radicals (
O 2 −
), that is when the device is subjected to reduction in gases, and the electrons are released back to MgO producing the growth of conductivity of the sensor device. H2S gas interacts with
O 2 −
radicals and liberates three electrons as compared to other tested gases. So, the higher change in conductivity in case of H2S gas will be produced when compared to other gases which produce a lower number of liberated electrons.

In summary, CDots@MgO and MgO sensor devices based on Schottky structure have been engineered for H2S gas detection. The spin coating approach was used to engineer the sensor devices. The fabricated sensor device exhibited a behavior like Schottky diode. The response of the fabricated device is tested for different reducing gases and it was found that the device is more responsive to H2S gas, owing to the decrease in the barrier height produced when the device is exposed to H2S gas as compared to other gases. Interestingly, under the exposure of 120 ppm of H2S, CDots@MgO sensor displayed response current (JH2S-Jair) value 11 times higher than MgO sensor at an external biasing voltage = −0.7 V. The sensor (CDots@MgO) shows a good stability for long-time continuous measurements as well as a satisfactory performance in humid conditions. The CDots@MgO sensor can be used for H2S gas sensing for 8 cycles without change in its response. The study proved that the strong effect of Tapplied on the response and crystallinity of CDots@MgO nano-particles up to 200 °C. SEM micrographs showed that the increase in the grain size of CDots@MgO nano-particles with increasing the applied temperature. It was concluded also that the schottky barrier height between the grains of CDots@MgO decreases and then increases with increasing Tapplied . The sensor showed a high response to sensing H2S gas at low operating temperature (30 °C). Finally, this effort introduces an invaluable methodology aiming at sensing the harmful gases by engineering a new gas sensor based on Schottky device of metal oxides decorated with CDots with high response, low power-consumption and high stability.
Ahmed Gamal El-Shamy: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ahmed Gamal El-Shamy received his Ph.D. degree in 2018 in the manufacturing and developing of polymer nano-composites. He has years of experience in the field of nano-composites, Thermoelectric power devices, Catalysis & Photocatalysis and EM Shielding Materials. Currently, he mainly focuses on the synthesis and application of Carbon Materials, Carbon Dots, Graphene, Graphene Oxides and Semiconductors as Gas and Bio-sensors.
